learning_outcomes.description: Apply promotional theory and strategic thinking to analyze case studies and design campaigns for specific target audiences.
Utilize social media tools to create multi-modal content that prioritizes clarity, concision, and high audience engagement.
Evaluate social media campaigns and peer performance using industry-specific metrics and tools.
Collaborate with colleagues to pitch communication solutions via structured oral presentations.
Defend strategic communication choices and demonstrate subject knowledge during live, spontaneous questioning.
Critically reflect on key industry themes, personal development, and individual contributions to group environments

assessments.assessment_title: CREATE: Video & Oral Presentation and Groupwork Reflection
assessments.task_description:
Assessment 1: (Create) Video & Oral presentation and Evaluation (30%) 

Part A: Video Presentation – due in week 5 tutorial 2026  

Part B: Oral Presentation – due in week 5 tutorial 2026 

Part C: Groupwork Evaluation – due 28 August 2026 @4pm 

Weighting: Part A 15% Group, Part B 10%, Individual Part C 5%Individual (30% Overall) 

_______________________________________________________________________________________ 

There are three parts to this assignment – Part A, Part B and Part C.  

Part A- Video Presentation (15 marks):  

Part A is a group task, and Part B and Part C are individual tasks. All tasks must be completed to pass Assessment 1. 

In groups of 4-5, you will create a TikTok or Instagram reel series (3 x two-minute reels) designed to educate a specific target audience about promotional culture. You will present this in a “thought leadership” style. 

In your groups you will select a current social media example that is applicable to course concepts from weeks 1-4 (which are all aspects of promotional culture). In your videos, you will analyse the example through the lens of relevant theory by demonstrating engagement with specific readings from the course content from weeks 1-4. 

In industry settings, it is standard procedure to work with colleagues that you don’t know. Use this task as an opportunity to practise working professionally alongside your ‘colleagues.’  

You will present your videos in class during the Week 5 tutorial. Then, you will undertake Part B which consists of an oral presentation and Q&A where you must discuss the rationale behind your video. 

Part B- Oral presentation (10 marks): 

Each student will receive an individual mark for Part B, determined partly by performance and partly by the quality of their response to spontaneous questions. 

After Part A, in the Week 5 tutorial you will give an oral presentation with your group where you will explain your rationale for the videos. All group members will be expected to speak for approximately two (2) minutes for a total presentation time of 8-10 minutes and respond to questions from the tutor. You do not need to create any slides/visual aids for this. 

Each group should cover the following aspects in your rationale 

  • Target audience: who is the target audience for this video? 
  • Strategy: what is your overarching plan? 
  • Goal: what are you trying to get your target audience to do or think? 
  • Objectives: what are you hoping to achieve through this video? For example, comments, likes, shares, click-throughs, views etc. 
  • Key Message: what is the message you want your viewers to take away? What do you want them to think about or discuss with friends? What change in behaviour or thinking are you trying to influence? 
  • Tactics: What is your communication and creative tactic to reach and engage this target audience? 
  • Theory: You need to meaningfully, verbally reference at least two relevant course readings/sources 


Part C- Groupwork Evaluation (5 marks): 

By Friday 4pm of Week 5, you must answer specific prompts about group engagement throughout the planning and production of your video series. Your responses should total 150 words. This will be submitted via Blackboard. 

Questions:  

  • What were the strengths of your group dynamic? 
  • What were some challenges you faced throughout the groupwork process? 
  • How did you resolve these challenges? 
  • What would you do differently in future groupwork settings? 


Recording of Oral and Practical Assessment  

  • All presentations will be recorded for marking purposes via recording facilities available where the assessment takes place (eg. ECHO360, Zoom, camera device)  
  • Recordings will be retained by the School of Communication and Arts for at least 12 months from the release of the final grade for the course.  
  • Recordings will be stored in a secure manner and will only be accessed by authorised school staff for the purposes of: 
  • Moderation of marking;  
  • Provision of feedback to the student(s) recorded; and/or  
  • Re-marking following a successful re-mark application.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Part A: Video Presentation – present in week 5 tutorial 2026
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: PROMOTE: Case Study Analysis & Peer Engagement
assessments.task_description:
Assessment 2: (Promote) Case Study Analysis & Peer Engagement  

Part A: Case Study Presentation – due in week 6-9 tutorials 2026  

Part B: Peer Engagement – due in due in week 6-9 tutorials 2026 

Weighting: Part A 20% Individual & Part B 15% Individual (35% Overall) 

_______________________________________________________________________________________  

There are two parts to this assignment – Part A and Part B. Both parts are individual tasks. 

Part A- Case Study Presentation  (20 marks): 

For Part A, in tutorials from Weeks 6–9 you will be allocated to present during one of your regularly scheduled tutorial sessions. Your presentation will be based on that week’s topic (e.g. self-branding, influencer culture, etc.). Alongside the other students presenting in your tutorial, you will deliver a 10-minute presentation. Afterwards, you will lead a 5-minute Q&A discussion. You must create one (1) slide that clearly presents your key analytical points and lists your references.  

For this task, you must select your own case study in which a social media creator engages in self-branding practices in partnership with a brand. Drawing on the course lectures and readings for your allocated week, you are required to critically analyse the creator’s use of self-branding and examine how it aligns with the associated brand. Make sure you are familiar with the readings ahead of time.  

There is no expectation that you argue whether the partnership was “good” or “bad”; rather, the focus is on applying course concepts thoughtfully and critically to your chosen example. Depending on your analysis, your presentation may function as a critique of influencer culture, an evaluation of promotional strategies, or an exploration of branding and identity practices within digital media culture. Your analysis should also identify and evaluate at least two ethical dimensions of your topic through social, cultural and/or political lenses. To do this, your presentation must demonstrate meaningful engagement with at least two course readings, as well as any additional relevant sources you have independently identified. 

You will also be graded on how thoughtfully you answer questions from peers and your tutor about your presentation. The idea is for you to facilitate a meaningful discussion based on your expertise of your specific case study.  

Part B- Peer Engagement (15 marks): 

Part B occurs while other students are presenting for their Part A.  When you are not presenting (including before or after your own presentation), you will be given a worksheet where you must respond to your peers’ presentations through handwritten commentary (in response to provided questions) as they are presenting. You will use your written commentary to engage in the Q&A session with the student who presented. 

You must take a photo of your completed worksheet and upload it to Blackboard by 4pm. It will be graded on how you: 

  • Demonstrate meaningful and nuanced engagement with peers’ presentations 
  • Demonstrate knowledge of course content 

Your tutor will collect your worksheets at the end of tutorials. 

Your best 2/4 worksheets will be counted towards your grade.


Recording of Oral and Practical Assessment 

  • All presentations will be recorded for marking purposes via recording facilities available where the assessment takes place (eg. ECHO360, Zoom, camera device)  
  • Recordings will be retained by the School of Communication and Arts for at least 12 months from the release of the final grade for the course.  
  • Recordings will be stored in a secure manner and will only be accessed by authorised school staff for the purposes of: 
  • Moderation of marking;  
  • Provision of feedback to the student(s) recorded; and/or  
  • Re-marking following a successful re-mark application.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Part A: Case Study Presentation – due in week 6-9 tutorials 2026
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.assessment_title: EVALUATE: Presentation, Evaluation Report & Peer Evaluation
assessments.due_date:
19/10/2026 - 23/10/2026

Part A: AV Presentation and Q&A – due in week 12 tutorial 2026  

Part B: Evaluation Report – due 4pm on day of tutorial presentation 2026

Part C: Peer Evaluation – due 23 October 2026 @4pm
assessments.task_description:
Part A: AV Presentation and Q&A – due in week 12 tutorial 2026  

Part B: Evaluation Report – due 4pm on day of tutorial presentation 2026

Part C: Peer Evaluation – due 23 October 2026 @4pm 

Weighting: Part A 20% Group, Part B 10% Group & Part C 5% Individual (35% Overall) 

_______________________________________________________________________________________  

There are three parts to this assignment – Part A, Part B and Part C. Part A and B are group tasks and Part C is an individual task.  

Part A – AV Presentation and Q&A (20 marks): 

In this assignment, you will be provided with performance data from a communications campaign and, working in groups of 4–5, take on the role of a consultancy that has implemented the campaign. You will analyse its performance and translate insights into actionable client recommendations. Groups will be randomly allocated as in professional settings. 

This task develops your ability to evaluate performance data, make informed decisions, and demonstrate value to clients and stakeholders. Post-campaign evaluation is a critical component of contemporary social media and digital marketing practice. You will present your findings as industry professionals, using campaign results to justify strategic decisions and propose future directions—reflecting real-world agency practice. 

Your group will use the provided PowerPoint template to present your key insights and findings in an audio-visual industry-style report deck. The deck should communicate your evaluation and recommendations clearly and professionally, as though it were being presented to a client.  

Your presentation should include:  

  • An evaluation of both Organic and Paid channel performance against the original campaign objectives (e.g. were targets met across key metrics including reach, engagement, click-throughs, conversions, etc.) 
  • An assessment of how evolving platform conditions such as algorithm changes or shifts in search behaviour may have influenced campaign performance and how results are measured 
  • Strategic recommendations and next steps for future campaigns, evidenced directly by your analysis of the performance data 
  • Walkthrough of the overall campaign performance, your evaluation, and your recommendations 

Your video presentation should be 10 minutes, and all groups will exhibit their video in the Week 12 tutorial. All group members need to prepare for a 5-minute Q&A discussion with the tutor after the video has been exhibited.  

You will be assessed on how well as a group you: 

  • Critically evaluate the provided data 
  • Utilise industry sources to support your evaluation 
  • Use campaign performance evaluation to inform future recommendations 
  • Align with compelling industry design standards
  • How well you respond to questions   


Part B- Evaluation Report (10 marks): 

Part B is a group task. Each group must submit one structured report that supports the video presentation. The structure will be provided to you, and acts as a written version of your presentation.  

Your report will be assessed on how you follow industry structure and style and communicate with clarity. 

Part C- Groupwork Evaluation (5 marks): 

Part C is an individual task. By Friday 4pm of Week 12, you must answer specific prompts about group engagement throughout the planning and production of your video series. Your responses should total 150 words. This will be submitted via Blackboard. 

Questions:  

  • What were the strengths of your group dynamic? 
  • What were some challenges you faced throughout the groupwork process? 
  • How did you resolve these challenges? 
  • What would you do differently in future groupwork settings? 


Recording of Oral and Practical Assessment 

  • All presentations will be recorded for marking purposes via recording facilities available where the assessment takes place (eg. ECHO360, Zoom, camera device)  
  • Recordings will be retained by the School of Communication and Arts for at least 12 months from the release of the final grade for the course.  
  • Recordings will be stored in a secure manner and will only be accessed by authorised school staff for the purposes of: 
  • Moderation of marking;  
  • Provision of feedback to the student(s) recorded; and/or  
  • Re-marking following a successful re-mark application.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit Part B and Part C via the TurnItIn submission link provided on the course Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Module 1 CREATE: Week 1 Lecture (Recording)

The lecture this week will be delivered as a video recording so that students can rewatch critical information on the assessment and course content. Content will cover how brands, governments, not-for-profit organisations and government policies shape advertising and media promotion. It is vital that students complete course readings and watch the first animated video on 'promotional culture' this week to prepare for assessment one.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L06

Content will cover how brands, governments, not-for-profit organisations and government policies shape advertising and media promotion. It is vital that students complete course readings and watch the first animated video on 'promotional culture' this week to prepare for assessment one.

learning_activities.topic:
Module 1 CREATE: Week 4 Lecture (In Person)

This seminar looks at the concept of labour and the work users perform when they engage with social media platforms, and how this differs from more traditional forms of media such as television. The relationship between labour and self-branding is explored.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02

The relationship between labour and self-branding is explored.
